coredata.dc:title: Emissive carbon dots derived from natural liquid fuels and its biological sensing for copper ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chunxia
Pan, Caiwen
Wei, Xiaoran
Yang, Fan
Wu, Wenjie
Mao, Lanqun
coredata.dc:description: Liquid fuels rich in aliphatic and aromatic carbon compounds are utilized as the carbon precursors for the production of carbon dots (CDs) for the first time. Herein we demonstrate the preparation of liquid fuels derived CDs (d-CDs and f-CDs) via chemical oxidation approach and the subsequent application for cerebral copper ions (Cu2+) sensing in rat brain microdialysate. The as-prepared CDs exhibits different morphology and photoluminescence properties depending on the different component of precursors. The luminescence of the as-prepared d-CDs can be significantly quenched upon addition of Cu2+, in which Cu2+ are trapped by the oxygen and nitrogen functional groups surrounding the emissive d-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs is sensitive to the concentration of Cu2+ with a linear relationship in the range of 0–4 μM, and a detection limit was estimated to be 0.039 μM. Simultaneously, we found out that the luminescent d-CDs manifests an extraordinarily high selectivity which can clearly discriminate Cu2+ from other interference species in aqueous solution and therefore substantially endows d-CDs as a fluorescent sensing platform for Cu2+. According to this nature, we employed d-CDs for the in vivo analysis of Cu2+ in the rat brain microdialysate and the measured basal level of Cu2+ was estimated to be 2.82 ± 0.16 μM (n = 3) even in the presence of other metal ions, biological substances and amino acids that commonly existing in the rat brain.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Natural liquid fuels
Biological sensing
Copper ions
In vivo

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing for Cu2+ based on d-CDs and f-CDs
Photoluminescence properties of d-CDs and f-CDs
Fluorescence inhibition of Cu2+ towards d-CDs
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of d-CDs towards Cu2+
Fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ in rat brain microdialysate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of d-CDs. Inset, HRTEM image (left) and size distribution (right). (B) AFM image of d-CDs.

(A) FT-IR spectra of diesel (black curve) and d-CDs (red curve). (B) Raman spectra of d-CDs. (C) XPS survey spectra of d-CDs. (D), (E) and (F) are high-resolution C1s, O1s and N 1s XPS spectra of d-CDs, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis spectra of d-CDs. Inset are the photos of the d-CDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV lamp at 365 nm (right). (B) Photoluminescence spectra under different excitation wavelength from 300 to 480 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of d-CDs toward different concentration of Cu2+ from top to bottom: 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 10, 20, 50,100, and 200 μM, respectively. (B) Plot of 1-F/F
0 versus the concentration of Cu2+. Inset, calibration curve of CCu2+ versus 1- F/F 0. F 0 and F represent the photoluminescence intensity of d-CDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. The error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent experiments.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra before and after addition of Cu2+ to d-CDs.(from bottom to top: 0.0, 50, 100, 200 and 500 μM). (B) Plot of F 0/F versus various Cu2+ concentration (0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, and 4.0 μM). F 0 and F represent the photoluminescence intensity of d-CDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. (C) Photoluminescence decay curve of d-CDs. (D) Fluorescence spectra of d-CDs (black curve) and d-CDs/Cu2+ versus different concentration of EDTA (0 μM, red curve; 20 μM, blue curve; 40 μM, pink curve). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Plot of 1- F/F 0 of d-CDs against different metal ions, total other metal ions (TM), total other metal ions and Cu2+ (TM/Cu2+). The concentrations of all the metal ions are 50 μM, except K+ and Na+ which are 1 mM. (B) Plot of 1- F/F 0 of d-CDs in the presence of different biological substances, total biological substances (TB), TB and Cu2+ (TB/Cu2+) and Cu2+. The concentrations of all the biological substances are 50 μM except glucose (1 mM), H2O2 (1.5 μM). (C) Plot of 1- F/F 0 of d-CDs in the presence of different amino acids, TA (toal amino acids), TA and Cu2+ (TA/Cu2+) and Cu2+. The concentrations of all the amino acids are 50 μΜ in the resulting mixtures.

Fluorescence spectra of d-CDs (black curve) with the addition of aCSF suspension (red curve), and with the addition microdialysis solution (blue curve). Excitation: 310 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation of d-CDs and fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ in rat brain microdialysate.

head.ce:title: Emissive carbon dots derived from natural liquid fuels and its biological sensing for copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of New Energy and Materials, China University of Petroleum (Beijing), Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of New Energy and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of New Energy and Materials, China University of Petroleum (Beijing), Beijing 102249, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, The Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences
Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, the Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Liquid fuels rich in aliphatic and aromatic carbon compounds are utilized as the carbon precursors for the production of carbon dots (CDs) for the first time. Herein we demonstrate the preparation of liquid fuels derived CDs (d-CDs and f-CDs) via chemical oxidation approach and the subsequent application for cerebral copper ions (Cu2+) sensing in rat brain microdialysate. The as-prepared CDs exhibits different morphology and photoluminescence properties depending on the different component of precursors. The luminescence of the as-prepared d-CDs can be significantly quenched upon addition of Cu2+, in which Cu2+ are trapped by the oxygen and nitrogen functional groups surrounding the emissive d-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs is sensitive to the concentration of Cu2+ with a linear relationship in the range of 0–4 μM, and a detection limit was estimated to be 0.039 μM. Simultaneously, we found out that the luminescent d-CDs manifests an extraordinarily high selectivity which can clearly discriminate Cu2+ from other interference species in aqueous solution and therefore substantially endows d-CDs as a fluorescent sensing platform for Cu2+. According to this nature, we employed d-CDs for the in vivo analysis of Cu2+ in the rat brain microdialysate and the measured basal level of Cu2+ was estimated to be 2.82 ± 0.16 μM (n = 3) even in the presence of other metal ions, biological substances and amino acids that commonly existing in the rat brain.

Schematic illustration of the preparation of d-CDs and fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ in rat brain microdialysate.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ions (Cu2+) is one essential element utilized in human body, but alternation of Cu2+ concerns with a large amount of physiological and pathological activities that involved in neurotransmitters and neuromodulators at different biological levels [1]. For instance, Cu2+ plays a vital role in the formation of nerve cells and also participates in the activation process of various metalloenzymes since Cu2+ acts as a redox cofactor in a variety enzymes, such as cytochrome c oxidase and tyrosinase [2]. In addition, long-term exposure to high levels of Cu2+ and insufficient intake of Cu2+ can generate reactive oxygen species, trigger oxidative damage to neutral system and lead to neurodegenerative diseases [3–5]. Moreover, serious neurodegenerative diseases are closely related to Cu2+ metabolism disorders and Cu2+ dyshomeostasis in the brain has been considered as one of the risk factors of neurological disorders [6]. Besides, Cu2+ varies greatly in the diverse area of brain and poses a great difficulty in real time monitoring. Consequently, to achieve this goal, in vivo sensing provides a convincing approach to integrate living samples in real time with spatial resolution. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a promising method for simple, sensitive, and in vivo tracking of Cu2+ in biological samples.

Honestly, biological sensing with fluorescence methods for Cu2+ detection offers an attractive approach which is advantageous to many methods including electrochemical sensors [7], wearable biosensors [8], atomic absorption spectrometry [9] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy [10,11] because of its high sensitivity, real-time measurement and safe detection [12,13]. Moreover, fluorescence methods are particularly useful in revealing trace amounts of target molecules in vivo owing to their discovery of functional fluorescent reporter that can view pathological events with high time and spatial resolution. Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, for example, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) with functional groups on the edges displays excellent photoluminescence characteristics [14]. Besides, the spectra varies from UV to NIR region through adjusting the ratio of the precursors, the nature of the agents, and by means of doping and modifying surface chemistry [11,15,16] as well as selecting the synthesis approach [17–24]. Up to now, various precursors including graphene oxide (GO) [25–27], carbohydrates [28], biomass waste [29], coal [30], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [31,32], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [33], organic dyes [34,35] and carbon black [36] have been utilized as carbon source to synthesize multiple CDs for in vivo imaging and sensing [37–39]. However, liquid fuels rich in natural aromatic hydrocarbons and heteroatom compounds could be considered as the preferable precursor for the preparation of CDs has not been explored. As far as we know, it is for the first time we demonstrate the preparation of CDs from readily available liquid fuels as precursors as well as their application in fluorescent sensing in rat brain.

Herein, we employ readily available liquid fuels as raw material to prepare CDs (d-CDs and f-CDs) via chemical oxidation approach and the subsequent in vivo sensing for Cu2+ in the cerebral system in rat brain microdialysate. The d-CDs and f-CDs were prepared by treating diesel and fluid catalytic cracking gasoline (FCC) with acid oxidation method, respectively. The obtained d-CDs evidenced uniform size, good water solubility and excitation-independent luminescence. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs was significantly quenched upon increasing the Cu2+ concentration, this suppressed fluorescence is probably due to the interactions of Cu2+ with the carboxyl groups and nitrogen containing functional groups surrounding the emissive d-CDs induced quenching. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs displays a sensitive response to Cu2+ with a detection limit reach up to 0.039 μM. According to this, the d-CDs were further successfully applied for analysis of cerebral Cu2+ in the rat brain microdialysate. This assay might provide a motivation for researcher to apply new carbon precursors to prepare fluorescent reporters by using economic liquid fuels but also present a new pathway for monitoring of Cu2+ in vivo. Scheme 1

The diesel and FCCare obtained from Lanzhou Chemical Research Center. H2SO4 (98%), HNO3 (68%), H2O2 (30%), NaOH, KCl, and HCl (37%) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF) was prepared by mixing NaCl (126 mM), KCl (2.4 mM), NaHCO3 (27.5 mM), CaCl2 (1.1 mM), MgCl2 (0.85 mM), KH2PO4 (0.5 mM), and Na2SO4 (0.5 mM) into mili-Q water, and the pH was adjusted to 7.3. The mili-Q water with an electrical resistance of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 was used in this work. All the other chemicals and reagents were used without further purifications.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were documented on UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Both fluorescence lifetime and photoluminescence quantum yield were carried out on the fluorescence spectrometer FLS980 (Edinburgh, England). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were operated on ESCALab220I-XL with a monochromatic X-ray source (Al-Kα line of 1486.6 eV). Transmission electron microscopy image (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were acquired on F-20 microscopy (FEI, USA). Atomic force microscope (AFM) imaging was acquired by Dimension Icon (Bruker, American). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was carried out on a FT-IR Microscope with KBr pellet (Frontier IR/FIR, UK). Raman spectra were conducted on Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution. Zeta potential measurement were recorded using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments).

d-CDs were prepared by chemical oxidation method with slight modification in accordance with previous literature [30,40,41]. In brief, 0.5 mL of diesel was added into sulfuric acid and nitric acid mixture (V/V = 3/1) in a microreactor and ultrasound for 20 min. Subsequently, the acid oxidation reaction was carried out at 120 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the mixture was neutralized and centrifuged to remove the solid product. Ultimately, the obtained liquid mixture was transferred into dialysis bag for 72 h (MWCO 500–1000Da) to obtain the pure d-CDs. Accordingly, FCC was treated with the above method to produce f-CDs.

The quantum yield of d-CDs and f-CDs were examined using an integrating sphere attached FLS980 spectrofluorometer. First of all, the d-CDs and f-CDs solution was diluted to keep the absorption intensity lower than 0.1 under 310 nm. Then the fluorescence spectra of d-CDs and f-CDs were gathered separately ranging from 300 to 700 nm and each of the quantum yields was calculated using fluorescence software according to the photoluminescence measurement. The experiment was implemented 3 times at the same time to acquire the accurate quantum yield value. Eventually, the photoluminescence quantum yields are estimated to be 5.92% and 3.64% for d-CDs and f-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing for Cu2+ based on d-CDs and f-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 980 μL of d-CDs and 20 μL different concentration of Cu2+ were mixed and allowed to keep stand still for 2 min at room temperature. Then the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs were recorded before and after addition of Cu2+ by fluorescence spectra. As for the interference experiments, metal ions, biological substances, and amino acids were added in parallel into d-CDs aquesous dispersion for fluorescence measurement. Stability experiments were carried out in fluorescence spectroscopy to monitor fluorescence intensity versus time. Similarly, the response of f-CDs towards Cu2+ was measured by using the same method.

All procedures of animals were approved by the animal ethics committee in Chinese Academy of Sciences, Institute of Chemistry. In vivo microdialysis was conducted in accordance with our previous study [42,43]. Briefly, adult male Sprague−Dawley rats (370–400 g, Health Science Center, Peking University) were feed on a fixed procedure. Then after the rats were anaesthetized with pentobarbital (345 mg kg−1, i. p.) they were placed carefully onto a stereotaxic frame. According to a standard stereotaxic procedure, we place the microdialysis guide cannulas into the striatum and then fixed it sequentially with skull screws and dental cement. Throughout the surgery, we should ensure the body temperature of the rats to keep above 37 °C. After the rats was recovered for more than 24 h, we inserted a microdialysis probe (CMA, dialysis length, 4 mm; diameter, 0.24 mm) into the rats striatum, perfused with aCSF with an appropriate flow rate for 1.5 h prior to use. Then, the microdialysate was collected for analysis.

Since many research results demonstrated that the different composition of precursors will afford distinct morphology and photoluminescence properties [25–30]. Liquid fuels rich in different component of carbon source and trace amount of heteroatom was considered as the ideal original precursor for the preparation of CDs. Herein, diesel is mainly composed of nearly 60% of saturated hydrocarbons and about 40% of aromatics. Whereas saturated hydrocarbons (aliphatic and cyclic alkane) occupy up to approximately 80% in FCC, leaving 20% of aromatics [44]. It should not be overlooked that although the nitrogen and sulfur impurities content is minimized during the processing of crude oil, there is still a certain amount of nitrogen impurities due to technical limitations [45]. However, the sulfur content in this raw material is extremely less due to its harmfulness and desulfurization process [46]. To examine the influence of different composition of liquid fuels on the behavior of CDs, d-CDs and f-CDs were prepared by using the diesel and FCC separately as the precursor under acid oxidation treatment.

TEM images of d-CDs and f-CDs are examined. Fig. 1 A indicates the as-prepared d-CDs displays an average diameter of 3.5 nm (inset, Fig. 1A). Furthermore, HRTEM image (inset, Fig. 1A) demonstrates that d-CDs have a good graphitic structure of high crystallinity with a lattice distance around 0.22 nm, which is similar to the (100) facet of graphite [47]. However, the average diameter of f-CDs was ca. 4.8 nm (inset, Fig. S1A), which is a little larger than d-CDs. And HRTEM of f-CDs shows a lattice fringe of 0.23 nm similar to that of d-CDs (inset, Fig. S1A). The morphology of d-CDs and f-CDs supported on mica were also evidenced by AFM images which indicate well dispersion and a comparative narrow height distribution with an average height of 1.9 nm (Fig. 1B) and ca. 4.1 nm (Fig. S1B), respectively. This is consistent with most of the reported graphene [41,48] and graphene oxide structures [40]. Compared with the reported CDs, the above TEM and AFM characterizations clearly demonstrate the formation of diesel derived CDs (d-CDs) and FCC based CDs (f-CDs).

The FT-IR spectrum of d-CDs is shown in Fig. 2 A. The broad bands at 3424, and 1723 cm−1 reveal the presence of O–H and CO functional groups at the edge of d-CDs (Fig. 2A, black curve), revealing a higher hydrophilicity of d-CDs compared with diesel [49]. Moreover, two sharply enhanced peaks at 1107 and 991 cm−1 appeared which are assigned to the C–OH groups suggesting the oxidation occurs and the oxygen-functional groups are formed during the process [50,51]. Furthermore, the peak at 1459 cm−1 which was ascribed to the bending of N–H [52] and the peak of 821 cm−1 that belongs to the N–H deformation vibration [53] can be seen in the diesel and d-CDs. While the new peak at 1258 cm−1 was attributed to C–N/CN stretching vibration appeared in d-CDs [53]. These various forms of nitrogen bonds arises from the presence of nitrogen compounds existed in the diesel during the processing crude oil [45]. In addition, the peaks at 2953 cm−1 and 2924 cm−1, 1608 cm−1, and 618 cm−1 corresponds to aliphatic C–H stretching, CC vibration, and C–H vibration, respectively, can be seen in both diesel and d-CDs, further indicating the aromatic skeleton remains [19,54,55]. Combined the above results, the as-synthesized d-CDs own hydroxyl, carboxyl and nitrogen containing groups and these functional groups is conducive to Cu2+ binding [56]. Similar bands can be observed from f-CDs, however, a decreased peak intensity of CC, CO in f-CDs was observed (Fig. S2A) in comparison with d-CDs, suggesting that the low content of aromatic compounds in FCC induced lower content of aromatic functional groups.

Raman spectroscopy was utilized to investigate the graphitic structure of d-CDs and f-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, two peaks were observed at about 1358 and 1595 cm−1 in the d-CDs, which belong to the D band (disordered sp3 carbon) and G band (well-ordered graphite), respectively. Obviously, the intensity of the D band is lower than that of the G band, and I
D
/I
G was 0.71 which is comparable with reported CDs. However, the D and G bands of f-CDs were shifted to 1362 and 1604 cm−1 (Fig. S2B), and I
D
/I
G was found to be 0.66 demonstrate a high degree of crystallinity than d-CDs, showing that d-CDs owns a comparatively rich surface chemistry. The presence of oxygen and nitrogen groups of d-CDs and f-CDs were also evidenced by XPS analysis (Fig. 2C–F and S2C–F). Fig. 2C displays three obvious peaks of C1s at around 285.0 eV, O1s at about 532.5 eV, and N1s at approximate 400.0 eV with an atomic percentage of 57.99%, 38.00% and 4.01% respectively. While these atomic ratio values turned to 60.62%, 36.23% and 3.15%, respectively in the corresponding regions of f-CDs. The high-resolution C1s spectra of d-CDs can be decomposed into four peaks (Fig. 2D), corresponding to sp2 carbons (CC, 284.6 eV), sp3 carbons (C–C/C–O/C–N, 285.2 eV), carbonyl carbons (CO, 286.3 eV), and carboxyl carbons (COOH, 288.7eV) [15]. Similar C1s spectra of f-CDs can be obtained from the XPS results (Fig. S2D). The O1s band of d-CDs are divided into two peaks for CO (531.7eV) and C–O (533.0 eV), respectively (Fig. 2E) and the total oxygen-containing groups occupy an atomic value of 38% which is higher than that of f-CDs (36%) (Fig. S2E). The deconvolution of the N1s band shows three peaks of d-CDs and f-CDs (Figs. 2F and S2F), located at 399.2, 400.1, and 402.1 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphite N, respectively. The presence of nitrogen containing element offers an electron donating amino groups and enhances the luminescence of CDs as well as increases its high chelating ability with metal ions [57,58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence properties of d-CDs and f-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the d-CDs and f-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the d-CDs (Fig. 3 A) shows two peaks approximately at 230 and 310 nm, which belongs to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 groups and n-π* transition of oxygen or nitrogen based groups, respectively [15,59]. The light yellow colored d-CDs aqueous solution emits strong blue luminescence under irradiated at 310 nm light (Fig. 3A, inset). Fluorescence spectra shows that the strongest emission intensity of d-CDs located at 430 nm when excited at 310 nm. Notably, in contrast to some reported CDs [41,60], the maximum emission peaks of d-CDs exhibited nearly excitation-independent, which may be due to the d-CDs are size uniformed and narrow surface chemistry distributions [61]. The UV–vis spectrum of f-CDs in Fig. S3Ashows the two absorption peak positions are approximately consistent with d-CDs, benefiting from inherent aromatic units of FCC. However, compared with d-CDs, the emission wavelength of f-CDs in photoluminescence spectra is slightly red shifted (436 nm) in Fig. S3B. This is probably because the large particle size offers narrow band gap and therefore induce redshifted wavelength [30]. Moreover, the excitation spectrum of the f-CDs shows two peaks with a position quite close to that of d-CDs (Fig. S4, d-CDs, red curve and f-CDs, black curve). These results confirm that the d-CDs and f-CDs both have similiar absorption structures and luminescent centers. In addition, the absolute quantum yield of d-CDs was estimated to be about 5.92% in neutral conditions while f-CDs affords only 3.64%. The higher luminescent efficiency of d-CDs possibly arises from the reason that diesel are equipped with more abundant aromatic compounds and much more functional groups as well as hydroxyl, and nitrogen containing groups on their surface, leading to enhanced fluorescence [62]. Therefore in the following experiments, d-CDs with strong luminescence was utilized for fluorescent sensing application. Furthermore, both d-CDs and f-CDs demonstrate excellent photostability since the fluorescence intensity keep almost constant even after excitation for 7 h (Fig. S5).

In addition, the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs and f-CDs in aqueous solution are sensitive to the pH value, both d-CDs and f-CDs show pH-dependent photoluminescence behaviors (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity of d-CDs increases gradually as the pH changes from 2 to 7, then decline quickly in alkaline solution when pH increasing from 7 to 12. Similar behavior have been observed for f-CDs when pH varies from 2 to 12. We speculate that the functional groups such as amino and carboxylic groups of CDs induced protonation and non-protonation dominate the pH induced fluorescence change [63]. In addtion, we observed that the pH change do not cause red-shift or blue-shift for both CDs. This pH-sensitive photoluminescence phenomenon agrees with most of reported luminescent CDs [64]. Taking into account that a physiological environment generally holds a pH value between 7 and 8, this kind of CDs with maximum intensity in neutral media is quite beneficial for biosensing applications such as in vivo sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence inhibition of Cu2+ towards d-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the special coordination interaction Cu2+ with carboxyl, hydroxyl and nitrogen groups on the CDs surface which will provide active sites for Cu2+ coordination [65]. Inspired by the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs to Cu2+, we employ d-CDs to analysis of Cu2+ and expect a sensitive response towards Cu2+. To validate Cu2+ are able to interaction with the d-CDs, the fluorescence spectra of d-CDs were measured upon addition of Cu2+. As depicted in Fig. 4A, d-CDs exhibit a strong fluorescence with maximum emission wavelength (430 nm) under the optimum excitation (310 nm), addition of Cu2+ induces a dramatic fluorescence quenching, revealing the interaction between d-CDs and Cu2+ take place. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs is Cu2+ concentration dependent. As the Cu2+ concentration increases, the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs sharply decreases. In Fig. 4B, we can see that in the range of 0–4 μM a linear relationship of 1-F/F 0 versus the concentration of Cu2+ can be obtained, and the linear equation is 1-F/F 0 = 0.006+0.069 C, R2 = 0.994 (n = 3), in which C denotes the concentration of Cu2+ (μM). F and F 0 represents the fluorescence intensities of d-CDs at 430 nm in the presence and absence of Cu2+, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.039 μM, which was much lower than that of f-CDs (Fig. S7) and most of the reported approach for Cu2+ detection [66,67]. This may be owing to the d-CDs holds abundant oxygen and nitrogen containing groups which can form stable complex with Cu2+, leading to enhanced fluorescence quenching [56,65]. Such a fluorescence quenching property endows d-CDs with potential application for Cu2+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of d-CDs towards Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: In general, fluorescence quenching mechanism of luminescent materials towards metal ions can be concluded into several aspects, including ion binding interaction, photon induced electron transfer process, inner filter effect, static quenching effect, dynamic quenching effect, and both simultaneously [68]. To gain more insight into the quenching mechanism, a series of experiments and reasonable reasoning were utilized to prove the quenching mechanism. Considering the prepared d-CDs have a negative charge owing to the modification of oxygen-based groups (eg, carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc.). Zeta potential was used to measure the electronegativity of d-CDs, the results indicates that d-CDs are negatively charged with −19.5 mV which is consistented with our previous suspection. However, when Cu2+ were added and the zeta potential of d-CDs-Cu2+ rises to −14.6 mV, indicating that Cu2+ interact with these negatively charged functional groups through electrostatic interaction and the increase the zeta potential. This variation can be also evidenced by the UV–vis spectra of d-CDs upon addition of Cu2+.

As expressed in Fig. 5 A, initially, d-CDs have a distinct absorption peak at about 300 nm. However, as the concentration of Cu2+ increasing, the absorption intensity increases (low concentration range), and the position is somewhat blue-shifted and the absorption peak at around 300 nm almost disappears (high concentration range). This phenomenon is possibly attributed to the formation of the d-CDs-Cu2+ complex, which destroys the original emitting structure of d-CDs. From Fig. 5B, we can observe that F 0 /F is linear with the concentration of Cu2+ within 0–4 μM, satisfying the linear relationship of the Stern-Volmer equation, suggesting that both dynamic and static quenching happens (supporting information). However, as shown in Fig. 5C, the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments show that the lifetime of d-CDs in average remains almost constant before (Fig. 5C, black curve, 4.99 ns) and after (Fig. 5C, red curve, 4.81 ns) the addition of Cu2+, revealing that dynamic quenching is excluded in this process. Therefore, we concluded that the quenching mechanism predominantly arise from static quenching because of Cu2+ coordinate with the oxygen and nitrogen containing groups on the surface of d-CDs to form the non-fluorescence complexes. As we all know, metal chelating agent ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) has strong chelation ability with metal ions and the chelation stability constant (logK = 18.8) between EDTA and Cu2+ are higher than other metal ions (Ag+, Mg2+ and Ca2+ etc.). Herein, EDTA was used to displace the Cu2+ from d-CDs-Cu2+ solution. Interestingly, despite the strong chelation ability between EDTA and Cu2+ and even under high concentration of EDTA, EDTA failed to restore the fluorescence of d-CDs (Fig. 5D), suggesting that the d-CDs and Cu2+ have stronger binding ability than EDTA. This maybe attributed to the d-CDs make the surface oxygen and nitrogen functional groups more strongly bonded to Cu2+. These are obviously different from the previously reported fluorescence quenching system of Cu2+-CDs [66,69].

To explore the possibility of d-CDs for sensing Cu2+ in physically environment, various substance are employed to investigate the interference effects on the target analyte. The experiments were conducted by adding metal ions, biological substances and representative amino acids, respectively to the above system. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity ratios (1-F/F 0) of d-CDs were recorded by adding metal ions (K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe3+) to the sensing system. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 A, only Cu2+ or Cu2+ containing mixed metal ions (TM) produce obvious supressed fluorescence of d-CDs, suggesting the high selectivity of d-CDs for Cu2+ in aqueous solution. Apart from Cu2+, other biological molecules displays a negative effect on the fluorescence of the d-CDs. Following, the influence of biological substances that existed in rat brain was investigated and we found out that the separate addition of biological substances even with all biological substances (TB) mixed together could not produce significant effect on the fluorescence of d-CDs, and the influence on the fluorescence intensity can be ignored (Fig. 6B). Only the addition of Cu2+ or into the TB mixture can produce the desirable quenching effect, further showing a high selectivity of d-CDs towards Cu2+ by detecting biological substances universally present in the brain. Moreover, the fluorescence ratio did not change after the addition of various kinds of soly amino acids or coexisting amino acids (TA) (Fig. 6C) except the addition of Cu2+. Taken all the above results together, it can be concluded that the d-CDs demonstrate an extraordinary high selectivity for Cu2+ over these interference substances even under their coexistence conditions. It is encouraging that d-CDs can sense Cu2+ in the coexistence of biological species, underlying the high selectivity of d-CDs for detecting Cu2+ and the great potential of d-CDs for real sample application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ in rat brain microdialysate
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the prepared d-CDs can determine Cu2+ with highly sensitivity and selectivity in the presence of various disturbances, we explored the possibility of utilizing d-CDs as a fluorescent unit for the analysis of Cu2+ in practical application. Specifically, a 20 μL of rat brain microdialysates was added in the present d-CDs sensing system, allowed to keep standstill for 3 min, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded subsequently. As shown in Fig. 7 , d-CDs (Fig. 7, black curve) are not affected by the addition of aCSF and indicate d-CDs is highly salt-tolerant (Fig. 7, red curve). Conversely, the fluorescence intensity of d-CDs (Fig. 7, blue curve) reduces significantly upon addition of rat brain microdialysate, indicating the above sensing works and the Cu2+ in rat brain microdialysates can quenching the fluorescence of d-CDs. Based on the linear regression equation described, the average basal level of Cu2+ in rat brain was calculated to be 2.82 ± 0.16 μM (n = 3) (Table S1), this value aggrees with previous reported literature [70,71]. The specific test values of Cu2+ with various methods are listed in Table S2. The above results indicate that the as-synthesized d-CDs can be successfully utilized as a potential sensing platform for monitoring Cu2+ in rat brain microdialysate.

In summary, liquid fuels derived CDs (d-CDs and f-CDs) were prepared by one-step chemical oxidation of crude oil products under acid oxidation approach. The property of the as-prepared CDs are dependent on the component of precursors and we found out that the diesel derived CDs (d-CDs) composed of higher amount of aromatic compounds is highly sensitive to Cu2+ possibly due to rich surface functional groups such as oxygen and nitrogen containing groups that provide sufficient active sites for chelating with Cu2+. Moreover, differs from the previous reported systems, the Cu2+ suppressed fluorescence of the d-CDs could not be recovered by using EDTA to displace Cu2+ from d-CDs-Cu2+ system. This is possibly due to the abundant functional groups of d-CDs displays higher coordination ability with Cu2+ than that of EDTA, although EDTA is a strong metal chelating agent for Cu2+. Furthermore, the d-CDs displays high sensitivity, selectivity, and strong anti-interference ability for Cu2+ with a das low as 0.039 μM. In addition, we employed the fluorescence assay for the analysis of cerebral Cu2+ in rat brain, which offers a powerful potential application in biological analysis.

Chunxia Wang: Writing - review & editing. Caiwen Pan: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xiaoran Wei: Investigation. Fan Yang: Supervision. Wenjie Wu: Investigation. Lanqun Mao: Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21804319, 21776302, and 21776308), the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum (Beijing) (Nos. 2462016YJRC027, 2462017BJB04, 2462015YQ0306).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120375.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots nanosensor derived from 3D nanomesh graphene frameworks and its application for fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ in rat brain

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Target-triggered switching on and off the luminescence of lanthanide coordination polymer nanoparticles for selective and sensitive sensing of copper ions in rat brain

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Huang, F. Wu, L. Mao, Target-triggered switching on and off the luminescence of lanthanide coordination polymer nanoparticles for selective and sensitive sensing of copper ions in rat brain, Anal. Chem. 87 (2015) 6834-6841.
B.R. Masters, Principles of fluorescence spectroscopy, Third edition, SPIE 2008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper(II) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Wang, Z. Gu, W. Lei, W. Wang, X. Xia, Q. Hao, Graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper(II) ions, Sens. Actuators, B-Chemical 190 (2014) 516-522.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A selective and accurate ratiometric electrochemical biosensor for monitoring of Cu2+ ions in a rat brain

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, Y. Han, F. Zhao, G. Shi, Y. Tian, A selective and accurate ratiometric electrochemical biosensor for monitoring of Cu2+ ions in a rat brain, Anal. Chem. 87 (2015) 2931-2936.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective electrochemical strategy for monitoring of cerebral Cu2+ based on a carbon dot-TPEA hybridized surface

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Shao, H. Gu, Z. Wang, X. Chai, Y. Tian, G. Shi, Highly selective electrochemical strategy for monitoring of cerebral Cu2+ based on a carbon dot-TPEA hybridized surface, Anal. Chem, 85 (2013) 418-425.
